Production Spark Calculator — User Guide

HPE DL385 Gen11 · 3-node Proxmox cluster · 64c / 768 GB / 9× 3.2 TB NVMe per node · Dual NUMA

1. Overview 2. Header & Scenarios 3. Diagnostics 4. Infra Params 5. Executor Params 6. Overhead 7. Budget 8. Executor Math 9. Storage & Config 10. Partitioning 11. Network 12. How It Connects

1. Overview & Purpose

The production Spark calculator sizes Apache Spark workloads running via YARN on a 3-node HPE DL385 Gen11 Proxmox hyperconverged cluster. Each node provides 64 CPU cores (two 32-core AMD EPYC sockets), 768 GB DDR5 RAM (24× 32 GB DIMMs), and 9× 3.2 TB NVMe drives. Storage is split between Ceph distributed object storage (RGW S3 endpoint) and Spark local scratch, with OSDs consuming NVMe drives and the remainder available for shuffle.

The calculator helps you answer: given this hardware, how many executor slots can I run, how large should each executor be, and will there be enough scratch space and RAM to avoid OOM failures? It models all infrastructure reservations (Proxmox, Ceph RGW, Ceph MON, Ceph OSD) before allocating anything to Spark VMs.

Key difference from the dev cluster

Production nodes have two NUMA domains per node — each of the two AMD EPYC sockets constitutes one NUMA domain with 32 cores and 384 GB of local memory. The calculator enforces a hard limit of 32 vCPUs per VM when NUMA pinning is enabled, preventing a VM from spanning both NUMA domains and incurring remote memory latency. This dual-NUMA constraint is the most important hardware difference relative to the single-socket dev cluster.

AttributeProductionDevelopment
Nodes33
Cores/node64 (2× 32-core sockets)32 (1× 32-core socket)
RAM/node768 GB (24× 32 GB DDR5)384 GB (12× 32 GB DDR5)
NVMe/node9× 3.2 TB6× 3.84 TB
SSD/node2× 480 GB (Proxmox OS)2× 480 GB (Proxmox OS)
NUMA domains/node21
Max vCPUs/VM (NUMA on)3232 (whole node)

2. Header — Scenario Management & Hardware Constants

The top of the calculator contains the scenario management bar and a read-only hardware constants strip. These provide context for all calculations below.

Scenario buttons

ButtonAction
SaveWrites all current slider values to localStorage under the key spark_calc_prod. Restored automatically on next page load.
Save ScenarioPrompts for a name and saves a named snapshot to spark_calc_prod_scenarios. Scenarios persist across sessions.
Load ScenarioApplies a previously saved named scenario, restoring all sliders and recalculating. Does not overwrite session continuity storage.
Delete ScenarioRemoves the selected named scenario from localStorage.
View ScenarioDisplays the raw JSON of a saved scenario in a popup window for inspection or copying.

Hardware constants bar

The blue strip below the title shows the fixed physical hardware parameters that the calculator treats as immutable:

FieldValueMeaning
PHYSICAL NODES3Number of Proxmox hosts in the cluster
CORES/NODE64Total logical cores per host (2× 32-core EPYC)
NUMA/NODE2NUMA domains per host; each domain = one socket = 32 cores
RAM/NODE768 GBMaximum installed RAM (24 DIMMs × 32 GB); slider can reduce this to model partial population
SSDS/NODE2480 GB SSDs used for Proxmox OS ZFS mirror; not available for Ceph or Spark
NVME/NODE93.2 TB NVMe drives split between Ceph OSDs and Spark local scratch

3. Diagnostics & Warnings

The diagnostics panel appears near the top of the calculator and displays colored alerts when the current configuration would cause problems. Warnings fire reactively as you move sliders — there is no submit button.

Warning types

OOM Margin Warning (red)

Fires when OOM_MARGIN < 4 GB. OOM margin is the gap between the memory slot allocated to an executor and the sum of executor heap + overhead + off-heap. If this gap is thin, the Linux OOM killer will terminate executors under memory pressure. Reduce executor memory or increase RAM/VM to resolve.

NUMA Boundary Warning (orange) — production-specific

This warning is unique to the production calculator because production nodes have two NUMA domains (one per socket, 32 cores each). It fires when vCPUs/VM exceeds 32 under either condition:

  • NUMA pinning ON: hard error — "NUMA boundary crossed — vCPUs span both sockets, disable NUMA pinning". A pinned VM cannot physically span both NUMA domains; Proxmox will refuse to start it or performance will be severely degraded.
  • NUMA pinning OFF: advisory warning — vCPUs will be scheduled across both sockets, incurring remote NUMA memory latency (~15–30% bandwidth penalty). Consider keeping VMs within a single NUMA domain for memory-intensive shuffle workloads.
Production-specific: The dev cluster has only one NUMA domain per node (single socket, 32 cores total). The NUMA boundary warning can only be triggered on the production calculator by exceeding 32 vCPUs/VM.

Scratch Space Warning (orange)

Fires when available local NVMe scratch per VM is less than 75% of the worst-case shuffle volume (DAILY_TB × shuffle_amplification). Spark spills shuffle data to local disk; insufficient scratch causes job failures. Add fewer OSDs or reduce shuffle amplification to resolve.

Executor Count Warning (blue)

Advisory when TOTAL_EXEC is very low (fewer than 3 total executors across the cluster). This typically means the configuration is underutilizing the hardware. Adjust VMs/node, vCPUs/VM, or executor cores.

4. Infrastructure Parameters

These sliders control how the physical hardware is partitioned between infrastructure services and Spark VMs. Every change triggers an immediate full recalculation.

SliderRange / DefaultWhat it controls
RAM per node (GB) 128–1536 GB, step 32
Default: 768 GB		 Total RAM installed per physical node. Each step represents one 32 GB DIMM. Moving this slider dynamically recomputes the ceiling of the RAM/VM slider in Section 5, proportionally rescaling the current RAM/VM value to maintain the same percentage. Use this to model partial DIMM population (e.g., 512 GB = 16 DIMMs installed). The annotation reads "24 slots × 32 GB DIMMs".
VMs per node 1–8
Default: 4		 How many Spark worker VMs run on each physical node. More VMs means smaller VMs; fewer VMs means larger VMs with more RAM and vCPUs each. Total VMs in the cluster = VMs/node × 3 nodes.
vCPUs per VM 1–64
Default: 16		 Virtual CPUs assigned to each Spark worker VM. With NUMA pinning enabled, values above 32 will trigger the NUMA boundary warning. Each executor within the VM reserves 2 vCPUs for OS + NodeManager, so effective executor vCPUs = vCPUs/VM − 2.
NUMA pinning Checkbox
Default: on		 When enabled, Proxmox pins each VM to a single NUMA domain (one socket). This keeps all memory accesses local to the socket the VM is scheduled on, maximising memory bandwidth. With 64 cores split across 2 sockets, pinning is strongly recommended for shuffle-heavy workloads. Disabling allows VMs to span both sockets at the cost of potential remote memory latency.
Ceph OSDs per node 1–9
Default: 7		 Number of NVMe drives per node dedicated to Ceph storage (object store / S3 endpoint). The remaining drives (9 − OSDs) become Spark local scratch volumes. Fewer OSDs = more scratch; more OSDs = more Ceph capacity. Each OSD also consumes 1 core and 3 GB RAM from the node reservation.
RAM/node drives RAM/VM ceiling: The RAM per node slider sets the maximum possible value for the RAM/VM slider. When you reduce RAM/node, the RAM/VM slider maximum is recalculated and the current value is rescaled proportionally. Always set RAM/node first if you are modelling a specific DIMM configuration.
NUMA pinning with 64 cores: Production nodes have two 32-core sockets. Enabling NUMA pinning caps each VM at 32 vCPUs (one full socket). If you need more than 32 vCPUs per VM, you must disable NUMA pinning and accept potential remote memory access penalties. For most ETL workloads, keeping VMs within a single NUMA domain is the right trade-off.

5. Spark Executor Parameters

These sliders control how each Spark worker VM is divided into executor slots and how memory is allocated within each slot.

SliderRange / DefaultWhat it controls
RAM per VM (GB) 64–(computed max), step 32
Default: 160 GB		 RAM allocated to each Spark worker VM. The maximum is computed dynamically from RAM/node minus all fixed infrastructure reservations divided by VMs/node. 2 GB within each VM is reserved for the YARN NodeManager; the remainder is divided into executor slots.
Executor cores 1–16
Default: 5		 CPU cores allocated to each executor within a VM. The sweet spot for JVM GC efficiency is 4–6. More cores per executor means fewer executors per VM but more parallelism within each task set. Fewer cores means more executors with less per-executor parallelism.
Off-heap (GB) 0–16
Default: 2 GB		 Native memory allocated outside the JVM heap per executor, used by Parquet readers and Apache Arrow for columnar encoding/decoding. Set to 0 if your workload is pure RDD or DataFrame without Parquet I/O. Increasing this reduces the available JVM heap per executor.
JVM overhead 5–25%
Default: 10%		 Percentage buffer between the requested executor memory and the JVM -Xmx setting, accounting for JVM internals, thread stacks, and GC metadata. YARN will kill executors that exceed their memory container limit; this overhead prevents that. 10% is a safe default; increase to 15–20% for GC-heavy workloads.
Executor core sweet spot: 4–6 cores per executor is the well-established recommendation for Spark on JVM. Below 4, you lose task-level parallelism. Above 6, GC pause times increase because the heap is shared across more concurrent tasks. The default of 5 is a good starting point for ETL workloads.
Understanding executors and off-heap memory

When Spark runs a job, YARN launches executor processes on your worker VMs — one or more per VM. Each executor is a self-contained JVM (Java Virtual Machine) process that gets its own slice of RAM and CPU cores. Tasks — the individual units of computation (“process partition 47 of the CSV”) — are handed to executors, which run them in parallel across their allocated cores. 

Worker VM  (e.g. 768 GB RAM, 64 vCPUs)
├── Executor 1  (e.g. 200 GB RAM, 5 cores)  ← JVM process
├── Executor 2  (e.g. 200 GB RAM, 5 cores)  ← JVM process
├── Executor 3  (e.g. 200 GB RAM, 5 cores)  ← JVM process
└── OS + NodeManager overhead

Each executor runs tasks concurrently — one per core — so a 5-core executor runs 5 tasks simultaneously.

Two separate memory regions inside every executor 

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                     EXECUTOR PROCESS                        │
│                                                             │
│  ┌───────────────────────┐   ┌───────────────────────────┐  │
│  │      JVM HEAP         │   │    OFF-HEAP (native)      │  │
│  │  spark.executor       │   │    spark.memory           │  │
│  │  .memory              │   │    .offHeap.size          │  │
│  │                       │   │                           │  │
│  │  • Execution memory   │   │  • Parquet column         │  │
│  │    (shuffles, joins,  │   │    buffers                │  │
│  │    sort, aggregation) │   │  • Arrow columnar         │  │
│  │  • Storage memory     │   │    encoding / decoding    │  │
│  │    (cached datasets)  │   │  • Decompression          │  │
│  │  • User code objects  │   │    scratch space          │  │
│  │                       │   │                           │  │
│  │  Managed by GC ✓      │   │  No GC — direct OS ✓     │  │
│  └───────────────────────┘   └───────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                             │
│  + JVM overhead (thread stacks, NIO buffers, JVM internals) │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

The JVM heap is where Java objects live — DataFrames being processed, shuffle buffers, cached datasets. The JVM’s garbage collector (GC) automatically frees unused objects. GC is a blessing (no manual memory management) and a burden: when it runs, it pauses all threads in the executor. On a heap loaded with large byte arrays — like a 3 GB Parquet column chunk — GC pauses can last seconds, stalling tasks and triggering YARN timeouts.

Off-heap memory is allocated directly from the OS, bypassing the JVM entirely. The GC never sees it, never touches it, never pauses for it.

Why Parquet and Arrow specifically need off-heap

When Spark reads a Parquet file it works through three stages: 

1. Read       — compressed column chunks arrive from disk or S3 / Ceph into a raw byte buffer
2. Decompress — Snappy / Zstd expand the bytes into raw column data
3. Decode     — raw bytes are converted into values Spark can operate on (ints, strings, timestamps)

These intermediate buffers can be gigabytes per column for wide tables. Placing them on the JVM heap would constantly trigger GC. Apache Arrow (the in-memory columnar format Spark 3.x uses internally) allocates these buffers off-heap — no GC pause when a 2 GB column buffer is freed, and native vectorized decoders written in C++ can operate directly on the contiguous memory.

The silent failure mode: setting off-heap to 0 while Spark’s vectorized reader still tries to allocate native buffers can cause container-kill OOM crashes that produce no Java stack trace — the OS kills the process before the JVM can catch the error. The symptom is an executor that disappears from the YARN UI with no exception logged.
WorkloadOff-heap recommendation
Reading / writing Parquet or ORC2–8 GB depending on column width and file sizes
Heavy joins, window functionsErr toward more — large Arrow buffers during wide aggregations
Pure RDD operations (no structured API)0 — off-heap is never used
In-memory DataFrame ops, no file I/O0 or 1–2 GB as a safety margin
CSV → Parquet → Snowflake ETL (this project)4 GB — starting point for 2 TB/day at current scale

6. Fixed Overhead Reservations

Before any cores or RAM reach Spark VMs, the calculator subtracts fixed infrastructure reservations from each node. These are displayed in a read-only table and are not slider-configurable (except for OSD count, which scales the OSD reservation).

ComponentCores reservedRAM reservedNotes
Proxmox hypervisor28 GBDom0 OS processes, VM management, ZFS ARC cache for the OS SSDs
Ceph RadosGW (RGW)48 GBS3-compatible object gateway; handles all ingest writes and Snowflake COPY INTO reads
Ceph MON26 GBCeph monitor daemon; maintains cluster maps and quorum
Ceph OSD × N1 per OSD3 GB per OSDOne OSD process per NVMe drive; scales with the Ceph OSDs/node slider

With the default of 7 OSDs per node:

Reserved cores = 2 + 4 + 2 + 7×1 = 15 cores
Reserved RAM = 8 + 8 + 6 + 7×3 = 43 GB

Available for Spark VMs = 64 − 15 = 49 cores
Available for Spark VMs = 768 − 43 = 725 GB RAM

7. Resource Budget

The resource budget section shows two horizontal bar charts — one for CPU and one for RAM — that visualise how each reservation layer is carved from the physical totals before the remaining pool is split across Spark VMs.

Each coloured segment represents one reservation category:

  • Proxmox (dark blue) — hypervisor overhead
  • Ceph RGW — S3 gateway
  • Ceph MON — monitor daemon
  • Ceph OSD — one segment per OSD, scaled by OSD count slider
  • Spark VM Pool (green) — what remains; divided equally across VMs/node

The numeric readout below each bar shows the per-VM allocation: vCPUs actually assigned and GB RAM per VM. This is the input to executor sizing in Section 8.

Why the bars matter: Increasing OSDs/node shrinks the Spark VM Pool visible in the green segment. If you add OSDs to get more Ceph capacity, watch the green segment to ensure the VM pool remains large enough for the executor count you need.

8. Executor Math

The executor math panel shows the computed sizing results in real time. All values are derived from the formulas below.

Core formulas

// How many executors fit in one VM
EXEC_PER_VM = floor((VCPU_VM − 2) / EXEC_CORES)
// 2 vCPUs reserved for OS + YARN NodeManager

// Total executors across the cluster (minus 1 for YARN cluster-mode driver)
TOTAL_EXEC = max(1, EXEC_PER_VM × TOTAL_VMS − 1)

// Memory slot per executor
SLOT_GB = (RAM_VM − 2) / EXEC_PER_VM

// Maximum executor heap before safety margin
MAX_EXEC_MEM = (SLOT_GB − OFFHEAP) / (1 + OVH_PCT)

// Applied 5% safety margin
EXEC_MEM = floor(MAX_EXEC_MEM × 0.95)

// Headroom between slot and actual consumption
OOM_MARGIN = SLOT_GB − (EXEC_MEM × (1 + OVH_PCT) + OFFHEAP)

OOM Margin

OOM margin is the most important safety metric. If OOM_MARGIN < 4 GB, the red OOM warning fires. A thin margin means the Linux OOM killer may terminate executor containers if any transient allocation spikes push memory consumption past the YARN container limit.

EXEC_PER_VM detail

The − 2 in VCPU_VM − 2 reserves two vCPUs for the Linux guest OS and the YARN NodeManager daemon. These processes must be able to run without competing with executor threads. Without this reservation, NodeManager heartbeat delays can cause executors to be declared dead and tasks to be rescheduled unnecessarily.

TOTAL_EXEC and the driver slot

In YARN cluster mode, the Spark driver runs as an ApplicationMaster inside the cluster, occupying one executor slot. Subtracting 1 from the product gives the count of executor slots available for actual data processing. The max(1, …) ensures the result never goes negative for edge-case configurations.

9. Storage Layout & Spark Configuration

NVMe storage grid

The storage grid renders one tile per NVMe drive per node, colour-coded by role:

  • OSD (blue) — drive dedicated to Ceph; contributes to S3 object storage capacity
  • Scratch (green) — drive reserved for Spark local shuffle and sort spill

Scratch capacity per VM:

SCRATCH_TB_PER_VM = (NVME_COUNT − OSD_NODE) / VMS_NODE × NVME_TB
= (9 − OSD_NODE) / VMS_NODE × 3.2 TB

Total Ceph usable capacity (after replication):

CEPH_USABLE = OSD_NODE × NVME_TB × NODES / CEPH_REP
= OSD_NODE × 3.2 TB × 3 / 3 // default rep=3
Scratch warning threshold: If scratch per VM falls below 75% of worst-case shuffle volume (DAILY_TB × shuffle_amplification), the orange scratch warning fires. Spark will spill shuffle blocks to local disk; running out of scratch space causes job failures, not just slowdowns.

Spark configuration output

The calculator generates a ready-to-copy spark-defaults.conf parameter block based on all current slider values:

Key parameters generated:

ParameterSource
spark.executor.memoryEXEC_MEM computed above
spark.executor.memoryOverheadEXEC_MEM × OVH_PCT
spark.memory.offHeap.sizeOFFHEAP slider value
spark.executor.coresEXEC_CORES slider
spark.executor.instancesTOTAL_EXEC
spark.driver.memorySized relative to executor memory
spark.local.dirScratch mount paths on NVMe drives

10. Parquet Partitioning

The partitioning section estimates how many Spark partitions will be created during CSV ingest and how many Parquet output files will be written to Ceph S3 for Snowflake COPY INTO.

SliderRange / DefaultWhat it controls
CSV input size 500–20,000 MB, step 500
Default: 6,500 MB		 Total size of daily CSV data to be ingested. This is the raw uncompressed (or lightly compressed) file size before Parquet conversion.
Parquet compression ratio 2×–10×
Default: 5×		 Expected size reduction when converting CSV to Parquet with Snappy or Zstandard compression. ETL workloads typically achieve 4×–6× compression on structured data with high cardinality repeated strings.
Max partition bytes 32–256 MB, step 32
Default: 128 MB		 Maps to spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes. Controls how large a chunk of CSV each Spark read task processes — this is the input-side control. INPUT_PARTS = ceil(CSV_MB ÷ max_part_MB). Smaller values = more read tasks = more parallelism at the cost of scheduling overhead. Larger values = fewer tasks = less overhead but larger per-task memory footprint. Entirely independent of Target partition size, which governs shuffle output rebalancing after AQE coalescing.
How Max partition bytes and Target partition size relate: These two sliders look similar but govern completely different stages of a Spark job.

Max partition bytes (spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes) is the input-side control. It determines how Spark splits your CSV files into read tasks — each task processes at most this many bytes. It has no effect on what happens after the shuffle.

Target partition size (spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes) is the output-side / shuffle-side control. After joins and aggregations produce many small shuffle partitions, AQE coalesces them back toward this target. It has no effect on the read phase. 

CSV files → [Max partition bytes] → read tasks → shuffle → [Target partition size via AQE] → output tasks

Both default to 128 MB, which is why they appear identical — but they are solving different problems at different points in job execution. Tune them independently: max partition bytes when read task sizing is the concern; target partition size when post-shuffle task distribution or output file count is the concern.

Partition count formulas

INPUT_PARTS = ceil(CSV_MB / MAX_PART_MB)
PARQ_MB = CSV_MB / COMPRESSION_RATIO
OUTPUT_PARTS = ceil(PARQ_MB / 150) // target ~150 MB per output file
Snowflake file size sweet spot: Snowflake COPY INTO performs best with Parquet files in the 100–256 MB compressed range. Files smaller than ~50 MB cause excessive metadata overhead; files larger than 512 MB slow the initial COPY parse phase. The calculator targets 150 MB per output part file.

11. Network Path Estimator

The network estimator models the three-segment data path from Spark workers to Snowflake in Azure and identifies the bottleneck link.

SliderRange / DefaultWhat it controls
Node uplink (Gbps) 1–25 Gbps
Default: 10 Gbps		 Each worker node's NIC speed for the intra-cluster and uplink segments. With 3 nodes, the aggregate available bandwidth from the cluster is up to 3× this value, but the model considers the per-node rate for the first segment.
Core switch (Gbps) 10–400 Gbps
Default: 100 Gbps		 Aggregation/core switch backplane or uplink capacity. This is the second segment bottleneck between the cluster and the WAN handoff point.
WAN / Core→Snowflake (Mbps) 100–10,000 Mbps, step 100
Default: 1,000 Mbps		 Outbound internet or private circuit bandwidth to Azure where Snowflake is hosted. This is typically the tightest constraint for cloud-egress ETL pipelines.

Bottleneck model

EFFECTIVE_BW = min(NODE_GBPS × 3, SWITCH_GBPS, WAN_MBPS/1000) × 0.80
// 0.80 = 80% utilisation factor (TCP overhead, retransmits, protocol framing)

TRANSFER_TIME_MIN = PARQ_MB / (EFFECTIVE_BW × 1000 / 8) / 60

The calculator shows which of the three segments is the bottleneck, the effective throughput in Mbps, the estimated transfer time in minutes, and how that compares to the available re-run window (typically overnight hours minus ETL processing time).

12. How Everything Connects — The Calculation Chain

This section walks through a complete worked example on production hardware, showing how changing a single slider propagates through the entire calculation chain.

Starting configuration

ParameterValue
Physical nodes3
Cores/node64 (2× 32-core EPYC sockets)
RAM/node768 GB
VMs/node4
vCPUs/VM16
NUMA pinningON
Ceph OSDs/node7
Executor cores5 (starting value)
Off-heap2 GB
JVM overhead10%

Step 1: Infrastructure reservations per node

Proxmox: 2 cores, 8 GB
Ceph RGW: 4 cores, 8 GB
Ceph MON: 2 cores, 6 GB
Ceph OSD: 7 cores, 21 GB // 7 × (1 core, 3 GB)
——————————————
Total: 15 cores, 43 GB reserved per node

Step 2: Available resources per node

Available cores = 64 − 15 = 49 cores
Available RAM = 768 − 43 = 725 GB

Step 3: Per-VM allocation (4 VMs/node)

vCPUs per VM = floor(49 / 4) = 12 vCPUs // note: slider is set to 16, but available is 12
RAM per VM = 725 / 4 ≈ 181 GB
Note on vCPU display: The calculator uses the slider value (16 vCPUs) as the input to executor math, not the derived available-cores figure. The budget bar shows the discrepancy. In practice you would set vCPUs/VM to match what the cluster can actually deliver — here, 12 vCPUs/VM would be realistic for 4 VMs on a 49-core-available node.

Step 4: Executor sizing with exec_cores = 5

// Using slider vCPUs = 16, RAM/VM = 160 GB (slider default)
EXEC_PER_VM = floor((16 − 2) / 5) = floor(14 / 5) = 2 executors/VM
TOTAL_EXEC = max(1, 2 × (4×3) − 1) = max(1, 2×12 − 1) = 23 executors

NodeManager reserve = 2 GB
SLOT_GB = (160 − 2) / 2 = 79 GB per slot
MAX_EXEC_MEM = (79 − 2) / 1.10 ≈ 70.0 GB
EXEC_MEM = floor(70.0 × 0.95) = 66 GB
OOM_MARGIN = 79 − (66 × 1.10 + 2) = 79 − (72.6 + 2) = 4.4 GB // above 4 GB threshold, no OOM warning

Step 5: Change exec_cores from 5 to 7

EXEC_PER_VM = floor((16 − 2) / 7) = floor(14 / 7) = 2 executors/VM // same count
TOTAL_EXEC = max(1, 2 × 12 − 1) = 23 executors // unchanged

// Memory sizing is identical because EXEC_PER_VM did not change
SLOT_GB = 79 GB
EXEC_MEM = 66 GB
OOM_MARGIN = 4.4 GB

// What DID change: each executor now uses 7 cores instead of 5
// More CPU parallelism per executor, fewer tasks needed to saturate
// spark.executor.cores = 7 in the generated spark-defaults.conf
Insight: When 14 is evenly divisible by both 5 and 7, executor count stays the same (2 per VM). Changing exec_cores from 5 to 7 in this case increases per-executor parallelism without changing memory allocation. To change executor count, you need to pick a value of exec_cores that produces a different floor(14 / exec_cores) result (e.g., exec_cores=4 gives 3 executors/VM; exec_cores=8 gives 1).

Step 6: NUMA check

vCPUs/VM = 16
NUMA limit (pinning ON) = 32
16 ≤ 32 → NO NUMA WARNING

With 16 vCPUs and NUMA pinning enabled, the VM fits entirely within one EPYC socket (32 cores). All memory accesses are local to that socket's memory channels. If you increase vCPUs/VM to 33 or higher with NUMA pinning on, the red NUMA boundary warning fires immediately.

Summary of the calculation chain

  1. RAM/node slider sets the ceiling for RAM/VM slider
  2. Infrastructure reservations (fixed + OSD×N) are subtracted from node totals
  3. Remaining resources divided across VMs/node
  4. Each VM's vCPUs and RAM feed executor sizing math
  5. EXEC_PER_VM drives TOTAL_EXEC and SLOT_GB
  6. SLOT_GB drives EXEC_MEM and OOM_MARGIN
  7. TOTAL_EXEC and EXEC_MEM populate the Spark config output
  8. NVMe drives not used for OSDs become scratch; scratch vs. shuffle check fires warning if needed
  9. Partitioning and network estimates use the Parquet output size independently
